name: Mordred
description: Mordred, the infamous betrayer of King Arthur, is a complex figure shrouded in ambiguity and tragedy. Often portrayed as Arthur's illegitimate son (usually by his half-sister Morgause, though some accounts vary), Mordred's birth is frequently prophesied to bring about the downfall of Camelot. He embodies the themes of treachery, ambition, and the destructive nature of fate. Raised in the shadows, Mordred's resentment towards Arthur and the established order simmers beneath a veneer of loyalty. He seizes the opportunity, or perhaps succumbs to his predetermined destiny, to usurp the throne during Arthur's absence while the King is campaigning abroad. This act of rebellion plunges Britain into civil war, ultimately culminating in the Battle of Camlann, where both Arthur and Mordred meet their demise. Mordred's motivations are often debated, ranging from a genuine belief that he is the rightful heir to a purely malevolent desire for power. Regardless, his actions irrevocably shatter the Round Table and usher in the decline of Arthurian society. He represents the internal corruption and vulnerabilities that can undermine even the noblest of ideals and the most powerful of kingdoms. He is a symbol of the consequences of unchecked ambition and the devastating power of prophecy. His legacy remains a cautionary tale about the fragility of power and the ever-present threat of betrayal, forever intertwined with the legend of King Arthur.
subtitle: The Traitorous Son of Arthur
companions: Sir Lancelot (sometimes an ally before betrayal)

keyMyths.title: The Prophecy of Mordred's Birth
keyMyths.description: Merlin prophesies that a child born on May Day will bring about Arthur's downfall. To avert this fate, Arthur gathers all children born on that day, but Mordred escapes the purge, fulfilling the prophecy.
keyMyths.source: Le Morte d'Arthur by Sir Thomas Malory

keyMyths.title: The Usurpation of the Throne
keyMyths.description: While Arthur is away fighting in Gaul, Mordred seizes the throne of Camelot, declaring himself king. He attempts to marry Guinevere, further solidifying his claim and enraging Arthur.
keyMyths.source: Le Morte d'Arthur by Sir Thomas Malory

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Camlann
keyMyths.description: Arthur returns to Britain to confront Mordred. The Battle of Camlann ensues, resulting in the deaths of many knights and ultimately leading to the fatal wounding of both Arthur and Mordred.
keyMyths.source: Le Morte d'Arthur by Sir Thomas Malory

sources.author: Sir Thomas Malory
sources.description: A comprehensive and influential compilation of Arthurian legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive and influential compilation of Arthurian legends.

sources.author: Geoffrey of Monmouth
sources.description: An early chronicle of British history that includes the earliest known account of Mordred.
sources.text: An early chronicle of British history that includes the earliest known account of Mordred.
